verses.text: God sends Nathan to David with a story: a rich man with vast flocks steals the one beloved ewe lamb of a poor man to feed a guest. The prophet baits the trap with a tale, drawing David in before he sees himself. Truth often slips past our defences best in a story.

God sends Nathan to David with a story: a rich man with vast flocks steals the one beloved ewe lamb of a poor man to feed a guest. The prophet baits the trap with a tale, drawing David in before he sees himself. Truth often slips past our defences best in a story.

God sends Nathan to David with a story: a rich man with vast flocks steals the one beloved ewe lamb of a poor man to feed a guest. The prophet baits the trap with a tale, drawing David in before he sees himself. Truth often slips past our defences best in a story.

God sends Nathan to David with a story: a rich man with vast flocks steals the one beloved ewe lamb of a poor man to feed a guest. The prophet baits the trap with a tale, drawing David in before he sees himself. Truth often slips past our defences best in a story.

David burns with anger: the man deserves to die and must restore fourfold! He pronounces swift justice on the villain — never dreaming the villain is himself. We are often harshest on the very sins we are blind to in our own hearts.

David burns with anger: the man deserves to die and must restore fourfold! He pronounces swift justice on the villain — never dreaming the villain is himself. We are often harshest on the very sins we are blind to in our own hearts.

Nathan strikes: 'Thou art the man.' God recounts all He gave David, and asks why he despised His word to take Uriah's wife and kill him. The mask is torn off. Sooner or later, God's word finds us out and names our sin for exactly what it is.

Nathan strikes: 'Thou art the man.' God recounts all He gave David, and asks why he despised His word to take Uriah's wife and kill him. The mask is torn off. Sooner or later, God's word finds us out and names our sin for exactly what it is.

Nathan strikes: 'Thou art the man.' God recounts all He gave David, and asks why he despised His word to take Uriah's wife and kill him. The mask is torn off. Sooner or later, God's word finds us out and names our sin for exactly what it is.

The sentence is grievous: the sword will never depart from David's house, and evil will rise against him from within his own family, openly. Forgiven sin may still bear bitter earthly fruit. What David did in secret, God says, will be repaid in the sight of all Israel.

The sentence is grievous: the sword will never depart from David's house, and evil will rise against him from within his own family, openly. Forgiven sin may still bear bitter earthly fruit. What David did in secret, God says, will be repaid in the sight of all Israel.

The sentence is grievous: the sword will never depart from David's house, and evil will rise against him from within his own family, openly. Forgiven sin may still bear bitter earthly fruit. What David did in secret, God says, will be repaid in the sight of all Israel.

David does not excuse or rage; he says simply, 'I have sinned against the LORD.' At once Nathan answers, 'The LORD also hath put away thy sin.' True confession, without defence, meets instant pardon — though the child conceived in sin will still die. Grace forgives; consequences remain.

David does not excuse or rage; he says simply, 'I have sinned against the LORD.' At once Nathan answers, 'The LORD also hath put away thy sin.' True confession, without defence, meets instant pardon — though the child conceived in sin will still die. Grace forgives; consequences remain.

The child sickens, and David fasts and lies on the ground, pleading with God for the boy's life. He prays as long as there is hope. Faith does not give up while God may yet relent; David pours out his heart, leaving the verdict in the hands of a merciful God.

The child sickens, and David fasts and lies on the ground, pleading with God for the boy's life. He prays as long as there is hope. Faith does not give up while God may yet relent; David pours out his heart, leaving the verdict in the hands of a merciful God.

When the child dies, David rises, washes, worships, and eats — to his servants' surprise. He explains: while the child lived he prayed, but now, 'I shall go to him, but he shall not return to me.' Faith both wrestles in prayer and bows to God's will, comforted by hope beyond the grave.

When the child dies, David rises, washes, worships, and eats — to his servants' surprise. He explains: while the child lived he prayed, but now, 'I shall go to him, but he shall not return to me.' Faith both wrestles in prayer and bows to God's will, comforted by hope beyond the grave.

When the child dies, David rises, washes, worships, and eats — to his servants' surprise. He explains: while the child lived he prayed, but now, 'I shall go to him, but he shall not return to me.' Faith both wrestles in prayer and bows to God's will, comforted by hope beyond the grave.

David comforts Bathsheba, and she bears Solomon, and 'the LORD loved him,' sending word to name him Jedidiah, beloved of the LORD. Out of the place of David's deepest sin and sorrow, God brings the heir of the promise. Grace can make the scene of failure the cradle of mercy.

David comforts Bathsheba, and she bears Solomon, and 'the LORD loved him,' sending word to name him Jedidiah, beloved of the LORD. Out of the place of David's deepest sin and sorrow, God brings the heir of the promise. Grace can make the scene of failure the cradle of mercy.

Joab, besieging Rabbah, sends for David to come and take the city himself, so the victory and honour will be the king's. Even rough Joab guards his master's glory. There is a loyalty that wants its leader, not itself, to receive the credit.

Joab, besieging Rabbah, sends for David to come and take the city himself, so the victory and honour will be the king's. Even rough Joab guards his master's glory. There is a loyalty that wants its leader, not itself, to receive the credit.

David comes, takes Rabbah and its great spoil, and sets the Ammonites to hard labour. The kingdom's outward triumphs continue even as its inward troubles grow. Success abroad cannot heal the wounds sin has opened at home; the two run on side by side.
